name: Gradient Boosting Trees (XGBoost / LightGBM style)
applicable_scenarios: handle missing values and mixed feature types out of the box (in modern libs)
Kaggle-style problems with tens of features and thousands to millions of rows
math_form: F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \eta \cdot h_m(x), \; h_m = \arg\min_h \sum_i L(y_i, F_{m-1}(x_i) + h(x_i))
python_template:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor, GradientBoostingClassifier

def fit_gb_regressor(X, y, n_estimators=500, lr=0.05, max_depth=3, seed=0):
    model = GradientBoostingRegressor(
        n_estimators=n_estimators, learning_rate=lr,
        max_depth=max_depth, random_state=seed,
    )
    model.fit(X, y)
    return model

def fit_gb_classifier(X, y, n_estimators=500, lr=0.05, max_depth=3, seed=0):
    model = GradientBoostingClassifier(
        n_estimators=n_estimators, learning_rate=lr,
        max_depth=max_depth, random_state=seed,
    )
    model.fit(X, y)
    return model
typical_cases: demand forecasting with many engineered features
common_pitfalls: high capacity — overfits without early stopping on a validation set
sensitive to learning_rate / n_estimators interaction; tune jointly
not GPU-friendly in sklearn; use XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost for scale
poor calibration out of the box — apply Platt / isotonic for probabilities
